content.problemStatement:
Given an undirected connected tree with **N** nodes, numbered from **1** to **N**, and rooted at node **1**, find the height of the tree.

For example, in the following tree, the height is **2**. (**Note:** The height of a tree is the number of edges on the longest downward path between its root node and any of the leaves.)




Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer NNN — the number of vertices.
The next N−1N - 1N−1 lines describe the edges. The iii-th of these N−1N - 1N−1 lines contains two space-separated integers uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​, denoting a bidirectional edge between uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​.


Output Format
Output on the single line, the depth of the given tree.
constraints: The given graph is always a connected tree.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N — the number of vertices in the tree. The next N‑1 lines each contain two integers u and v denoting an undirected edge between vertices u and v. The tree is rooted at vertex 1.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the height of the tree – the number of edges on the longest path from the root (vertex 1) to any leaf.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The longest root‑to‑leaf paths are 1‑3‑4, 1‑3‑5, 1‑3‑6, 1‑2‑7 and 1‑2‑8, each containing 2 edges, so the height is 2.
The longest paths are 1‑2‑4 and 1‑2‑5, each with 2 edges.
Both leaves are directly connected to the root, giving height 1.

solution.approach: Root the tree at vertex 1. Perform a depth‑first search (or BFS) from the root while keeping the current depth. The maximum depth reached during the traversal equals the height. Because the graph is a tree, each vertex is visited exactly once, giving linear time. No additional preprocessing is required.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the adjacency list and recursion/stack overhead.
